Bianchi, Otávio
coredata.dc:description: In this study, photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by thermal pyrolysis of the microalgae spirulina without any surface passivating agent. The CQDs showed ~10 nm average size and exhibited blue photoluminescence emission around 450 nm under 340–400 nm excitation wavelength. A quantum yield of 23.5% was attained at 300 °C for 2 h. Thermodynamic and kinetic investigations about pyrolysis reaction showed that CQDs synthesis occurs slowly and non-spontaneously, with a mean activation energy of 192.6 kJ/mol. The final product showed a broad range of solubility, besides being efficient for lentil seed growth when dispersed in 0.05–0.1 mg/mL aqueous solutions. The CQDs reported here may be considered a promising renewable bio-based fertilizer for crop species in the sustainable agriculture field.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by thermal pyrolysis of the microalgae spirulina without any surface passivating agent. The CQDs showed ~10 nm average size and exhibited blue photoluminescence emission around 450 nm under 340–400 nm excitation wavelength. A quantum yield of 23.5% was attained at 300 °C for 2 h. Thermodynamic and kinetic investigations about pyrolysis reaction showed that CQDs synthesis occurs slowly and non-spontaneously, with a mean activation energy of 192.6 kJ/mol. The final product showed a broad range of solubility, besides being efficient for lentil seed growth when dispersed in 0.05–0.1 mg/mL aqueous solutions. The CQDs reported here may be considered a promising renewable bio-based fertilizer for crop species in the sustainable agriculture field.

ce:section.ce:para: The increased demand for organic foods and more eco-sustainable choices in recent years has stimulated the biostimulant market worldwide. In this context, carbon-based nanomaterials have emerged as eco-friendly technology for enhancing crop yield. As a novel member of the carbon-based nanomaterials family, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) emerge, which are nanoparticles easily obtained from inexpensive raw materials that exhibit fluorescence and other unique properties. CQDs have a typical size of less than 10 nm, with a core composed of carbon. This core can be superficially functionalized and tailor-made properties can be obtained for specific applications [1,2]. These materials can be applied in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, biomedical materials, sensors, printing inks, fingerprinting, photocatalysis, and light-emitting diodes [1,3,4]. Recently, CQDs have attracted significant interest in agriculture to improve plant growth and production, and considerable progress has been made in this area [5–10]. Several strategies have been reported for CQDs syntheses, such as laser irradiation, electrochemical etching, microwave/ultrasonic, hydrothermal/acidic oxidation routes, arc discharge, plasma treatment, among others [1,3,11,12]. Alternative green routes for synthesizing non-food renewable raw-based CQDs are considered in the synthetic methods [13–15].

Spirulina platensis is among the most abundant microalgae biomass species [16] that can be cultivated in adverse conditions, including non-potable water. Spirulina does not compete for fertile lands with traditional crops and reduces greenhouse gases in the atmosphere via photosynthesis [17,18]. It is high-protein biomass (~65%, dry weight) and low-lipid and carbohydrates (about 10–20%) [16,19]. Like other biomasses, spirulina can also be used as a raw material for the production of CQDs. Interestingly, the nitrogen content of spirulina (9.6%, dry weight) is significantly higher than that found in other biomasses and macroalgae species, like sugarcane bagasse (0.7%) [20], corn cob (1.3%), rice straw (0.8%), rice husk (0.8%) [21], Gracilaria gracilis (5.3%), and Enteromorpha. intestinalis (3.1%) [16]. The N-containing protein in spirulina acts as a source for N-doping in the production of improved quantum yield (QY) CQDs [12,17,22]. Also, algae biomass contains P, K, and Mg minerals in concentrations tenfold higher than lignocellulosic biomasses [23], influencing vegetable crops growth [9,24]. The hydrophilic groups on CQDs surface may retain and slowly release micronutrients and water inside the plants' xylem vessels, stimulate cultivation growth, demonstrate antifungal activities, and induce stress resistance to plants [5–8]. In this context, the supply of CQDs from abundant renewable sources such as spirulina can act as fertilizers and may contribute to the food security of future generations.

Among the strategies for obtaining CQDs, pyrolysis stands out for being a simple method, yielding high purity products and low residue contents. It can be used to convert algae rests into CQDs. The final pyrolysis product is called biochar, composed essentially of aromatic carbon and many minerals from the raw material [25,26]. However, pyrolysis may result in the loss of functional groups initially present in biochar, including oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH, –NH2) [25,27]. This phenomenon is aggravated by increased time and pyrolytic temperature, resulting in CQDs with a lower QY [26]. According to Chen et al. [28], low pyrolytic temperatures (<150 °C) had little influence on surface functional groups of pine needle biochar. However, the number of such groups decreased significantly in organic matter decomposed at higher pyrolytic temperatures (250–350 °C). Parnaudeau and Dignac [29] showed that the algae's proteins could be decomposed into amine or nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds at pyrolysis temperatures of 300–500 °C, contributing to N-dopants in biochar. Thus, the reduction in surface functional groups (–OH, –COOH, and –NH2) caused by the high-temperature treatment will directly affect CQDs polarity and, consequently, the solubility of these nanoparticles.

Due to the different applications of CQDs, it is essential to comprehend their behavior in solution, which can be represented by their solubility parameter (δ). This parameter is dependent on the nature and surface functional groups of these nanoparticles [30]. It indicates the solubility or dispersion of a solute in different solvents, as well as the interaction between two materials. Thus, knowing δ, we can define the most suitable solvent for the desired system application. However, to date, no studies are reporting the low-temperature pyrolysis of spirulina biomass for CQDs production or the effect of the remaining surface groups of CQDs on the solubility of these nanoparticles in different solvents. Also, the impact of using these nanoparticles on plant growth has not yet been studied. Therefore, this study aims to explore a more accessible and ecological synthetic approach for the recycling and converting spirulina residues into N-doped CQDs with a high QY. The pyrolysis performance of biomass was investigated using thermogravimetric analysis (TG), and the as-obtained CQDs were characterized by infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and fluorescence spectroscopies. We also investigated the dispersion behavior of CQDs in twenty solvents of different polarities and their effects on plant growth. We believe that spirulina-based CQDs can be used efficiently as renewable biofertilizers for crop species in the sustainable agriculture field. Furthermore, based on prior knowledge of the solubility parameters of these nanoparticles, it will be easier to map other applications for CQDs, contributing to a sustainable bioeconomy.

Spirulina biomass (dried-powder) was purchased from Galena Química e Farmacêutica LTDA (Campinas, Brazil). Quinine sulfate was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). The lentil seeds (Lens culinaris) were purchased from the local market. All solvents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ.cm) was used in all experiments.

CQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of spirulina under a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Six experimental conditions were tested: 200 °C for 2 h, 6 h and 12 h and 300 °C for 2 h, 6 h and 12 h. After carbonization, the fine powder (also called biochar) was dispersed in water (20 mL), and then centrifuged at 13000 rpm for fifteen minutes to remove suspended solid impurities. Finally, the CQDs were obtained by evaporation of the solvent. The effects of the conditions employed for CQDs synthesis (reaction temperature and reaction time) on their QY were studied using Box-Behnken factorial design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure, size distribution and photoluminescence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the spirulina biomass and the pyrolysis products produced at different conditions (i.e., CQDs) were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Impact 400 spectrometer (4000–400 cm−1, 64 scans, and 4 cm−1 resolution) using attenuated total reflectance mode (ATR, diamond at 45°). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were performed on an Omicron EA 125 model X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, with AlKα radiation operating at 1486.6 eV.

The CQDs' morphology was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). TEM images were performed on a JEOL JEM-1011 transmission electron microscope with 100 kV accelerating voltage. AFM images were performed on a Dimension Icon (Bruker) equipped with a scanasyst-air probe (Bruker). Before analysis, drops of a dilute aqueous solution of the CQDs (0.05 mg/L) were deposited on a 300-mesh carbon-coated copper grid (Ted Pella Inc-USA) for TEM and onto silicon substrates for AFM.

The size distribution of CQDs was determined at 25 °C using the dynamic light scattering technique (DLS; Delsa™ Nano Series, Beckman Coulter) equipped with a laser diode (30 mW, λ = 658 nm) and light source at a 165° dispersion angle. The size distribution was reported in terms of the particle volume density (% volume). All the experimental runs were repeated at least three times with no less than 70 accumulation times for each measurement. Zeta potential was carryout in Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, UK).

The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained in a spectrophotometer (Evolution 60S Thermo Scientific) from 200 to 800 nm at a wavelength step of 1 nm. The photoluminescence emission (PL) spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer LS 45 fluorescence spectrometer using an excitation wavelength at 360 nm to obtain the integrated fluorescence intensity (area under the PL curve between 280 and 700 nm wavelength). For all experiments, a 10 mm path-length quartz cuvette was used.

The quantum yield (Φ) of the CQDs was measured using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Φ = 54%) as a reference standard [12]. Briefly, the absorbance for six different concentrations of the standard and the CQDs samples at the excitation wavelengths and the fluorescence spectra of the exact solutions were measured, respectively. The PL emission spectra of quinine sulfate and CQDs solutions were also recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm to obtain the integrated fluorescence intensity (area measurement under the emission peak). Then, curves of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance were plotted, being the data treated by linear regression with an intercept at zero. The absolute values were estimated according to Eq. (1):

For these aqueous solutions, ηS/ηT = 1. To prevent the re-absorption effect, the absorbances of all samples in 10 mm cuvette were kept under 0.1 at 360 nm excitation wavelength (λex).

The variables, activation energy (ΔH*), activation entropy (ΔS*), and free activation energy (ΔG*), were calculated at T = T

The solubility of CQDs was evaluated in twenty different solvents. For each dispersion of 1 mg/mL, CQDs powder was placed in 3 mL glass vials before adding the solvent. The mixtures were then ultrasonicated (40 kHz, 110 W) for 10 min. If the CQDs powder persisted inhomogeneous after the ultrasonic procedure, the solvent was considered incompatible or unsuitable solvent. The solvents for which CQDs were well dispersed after one hour of sonication have been classified as suitable or compatible. The HSP [34] for CQDs was estimated by fitting the dispersion data through the algorithm described by Gharagheizi [35]. This procedure fits the dispersion data to a sphere with the center defined by the dispersive (δD), dipolar (δP), and hydrogen bond (δH) contributions of CQDs, besides a radius R0 indicative of the maximum solution tolerance. Only suitable solvents could lie within the sphere, as the other ones are found outside it, according to Zuaznabar-Gardona and Fragoso [30].

The experiment was conducted following Wang et al. [24] with adaptations. Briefly, lentil seeds of similar size were selected (twenty seeds for each group), soaked in pure water, and treated with different concentrations of CQDs solutions (0.05–0.5 mg/mL) in beaker flasks. The seeds were allowed to germinate at 28 °C, without illumination, for three days. Pure water as the control group and the CQDs solution as experimental groups were renewed every day. After three days, the beakers containing lentil plantules were transferred to a growth chamber with simulated sunlight (6400 K intensity) for 12 h each day. The chamber's relative humidity and temperature were maintained at 71.5% and 28 °C, respectively. Germination and seed growth was monitored for three days. After six days, lentil sprouts were harvested, root and stem lengths were measured, and the biomass of fresh lentil sprouts was determined. To ensure the accuracy of experimental data, all tests were repeated at least three times.

The CQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis reaction under temperatures ranging from 200 °C to 300 °C and intervals from 2 h to 12 h. The response surface methodology (Fig. 1a) was used to model and optimize the results obtained at the different pyrolysis conditions employed. The lower QYs for pyrolyzed spirulina were obtained at longer reaction times and lower temperatures. The maximum QY value (23.5%) was achieved for a pyrolysis temperature and time of 300 °C and 2 h, respectively. The pyrolysis led to the loss of the surface functional groups of CQDs at high temperatures (300 °C) and long-time reactions (12h). In contrast, at 200 °C, carbonization on the polar functional groups was minimized, which could be observed by FTIR vibration modes (Fig. 1b). The following bands have been detected in the FTIR spectrum of the raw microalgae biomass: OH/NH (3400–3200 cm−1, associated with the presence of water, alcohol, phenol, and amine); CH stretching (2960 cm−1, related to CH2 and CH3 groups); aromatic conjugated CO, CC (1648 cm−1); NH bending and CN stretching (1520 cm−1, associated to amide in proteins); COH phenolics (1270 cm−1); CO stretch of carboxylate ions (1375 cm−1); CH bending (1430 cm−1); CO single bond (1093 cm−1); and aromatic CH (904 cm−1) functional groups or chemical bonds [27,36,37]. By comparing the FTIR spectra of CQDs and spirulina raw material, it is possible to observe that the carbonization process led to the release of volatile compounds and the breaking of chemical bonds, changing the composition of the functional groups. The higher temperature used led to a decrease in several bands, such as those at 3273 cm−1 (OH stretching) and 2920 cm−1 (CH stretching). High-temperature pyrolysis can cause the transition from aliphatic to aromatic structure, aromatic ring formation, and H deformation [38]. A significant difference in biochar and raw spirulina biomass structures was observed for the aminoacids bands, which disappeared after pyrolysis. In the spectral window of CH and NH groups, just a broadband associated with CC interaction was perceptible. This change indicated an increase in aromatic groups and the degradation of amino acids during pyrolysis [27,36]. The peaks between 1300 cm−1 and 1000 cm−1 (OCO stretching) were attributed to the conversion of microalgae biomass' cellulose component [36]. However, when comparing the biochars obtained at 200 °C and 300 °C, we observe that the principal differences are related to the alkane and aromatic groups. Similar CQDs spectra were also observed for other species of algae [12,26].

The XPS technique was performed to complement the information regarding CQDs' surface functional groups and elemental states. Three peaks at 286.1 eV, 401.2 eV, and 533.3 eV were detected in the full XPS spectra corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. 2a) [37]. The C 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into four peaks, related to CC/CC (285.1 eV), sp3 carbon (CO/CN, 286.6 eV), carbonyl carbons (CO, 287.6 eV), and carboxyl carbons (COOH, 289.0 eV) (Fig. 2b) [17]. The N 1 s spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.2 eV, 401.1 eV, and 401.9 eV, representing CNC, NH and, CN chemical environments, respectively (Fig. 2c) [12]. The O 1 s spectra presented two peaks: 532.9 eV (CO) and 534.9 eV (COC/COH) (Fig. 2d) [17]. Both XPS and FTIR results elucidated the probable chemical composition of the synthesized CQDs.

After pyrolysis, the size of the CQDs produced at the best synthesis condition (300 °C for 2 h) was determined by the AFM, TEM and DLS techniques. The CQDs presented a uniform size and a well-dispersed distribution with a mean diameter of 10.9 ± 8.2 nm by DLS. The TEM image (Fig. 3a) and AFM images with associated height analyses (Fig. 3b) indicated that the CQDs are well dispersed and have a spherical shape with an average size of ~10 nm. These results confirmed the production of spirulina-based CQDs by the pyrolysis method herein described.

Fig. 4 shows the TG and differential thermogravimetric (DTG) curves obtained at four different heating rates for the spirulina. A mass loss of 9.6 wt% was observed from 30 to 180 °C, corresponding to vapor moisture loss and degradation of low molar mass compounds [19]. The most significant mass loss (70.4 wt%) occurred at 180–510 °C, related to the pyrolysis of carbohydrates (200–270 °C), proteins (320–450 °C), and lipids (200–450 °C) [39]. Above 510 °C, microalgae biomass weight continued to decrease owing to the degradation of fixed carbon [37]. The final residue was 20 wt% from non-volatile matter.

The kinetics parameter was determined by KAS and FR isoconversional methods. Table 1 depicts the dependency of E
a and pre-exponential factors on conversion degree. The mean E
a values were 198.67 kJ/mol and 179.59 kJ/mol for FR and KAS methods, respectively. These methods were in good agreement with each other. When the conversion rate α increased from 0.1 to 0.7, E
a of spirulina increased continuously from 76.09 kJ/mol to 276.74 kJ/mol, indicating that the residue was becoming harder to break down. The first mass loss stage of spirulina is related to water evaporation and low molar mass volatiles with low E
a . Then occurs the cleavage of macromolecules (proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates). Finally, the high value of E
a was related to the decomposition of charcoal [39]. Bach and Chen [40] discussed the pyrolysis reactions of the green microalgae Chlorella vulgaris that parallel reactions may occur. The biomass pyrolysis involves two main reactions: one related to the degradation of carbohydrates and proteins (200–450 °C) and the other associated with lipids decomposition (200–500 °C). For distinct biomasses, the decomposition of secondary compounds is likely to alter the behavior of the primary reaction, which limits the comparison of the kinetic parameters for biomass components.

Thermodynamic parameters are related to kinetic ones. Therefore, to calculate the thermodynamic parameters for pyrolysis of spirulina, the transition state theory was adopted. The results were: ΔH* = 165.3 kJ/mol (T
p = 200 °C), ΔS* = −160.7 J/mol.K (T
p = 200 °C), ΔG* = 214.3 kJ/mol (T
p = 200 °C), ΔH* = 163.9 kJ/mol (T
p = 300 °C), ΔS* = −151.8 J/mol.K (T
p = 300 °C), and ΔG* = 250.9 kJ/mol (T
p = 300 °C). Similar values have been found in the literature [19].

The negative values for entropy ΔS* indicate that the degradation process occurs slowly, and the activated complex obtained is more structured or organized than the initial spirulina [19]. On the other hand, the positive value for ΔH* indicates an endothermic reaction (Fig. 4). Additionally, the positive value of ΔG* indicates the non-spontaneous process of CQDs formation, justifying the need for an external heat supply.

The synthesis of the CQDs at 200 °C and 300 °C exhibited thermodynamic values relatively close to each other. However, at 300 °C, more energy is available in the system to form CQDs and remove degradation products, giving greater uniformity to the nanoparticles formed [19].

The photoluminescence properties of the spirulina-derived CQDs were investigated through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 5a, the optical absorption peak of the CQDs was observed in the UV region with maximum absorbance at 250 nm and a tail extending into the visible range. According to previous studies [26,41], the absorption at 250 nm is ascribed to n–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds. The tail extending into the visible range is attributed to n − π* transition of CO and CN bonds. The CO groups may originate from the surface of CQDs, while CC groups from the core. The synthesized CQDs show maximum fluorescent emission at 450 nm with QY 23.5% under excitation at 360 nm. The CQDs solution obtained at 300 °C for 2 h exhibited light yellow and intense fluorescent blue colors when observed under visible and UV light (at excitation of 360 nm), respectively (see the inset in Fig. 5a). Fig. 5b shows the PL emission spectra of the CQDs excited at different wavelengths (320–440 nm).

A strong PL emission peak at 440 nm was observed for an excitation wavelength at 360 nm. The PL emission peak shifted toward higher wavelengths as higher excitation wavelengths have been employed. The mechanism of CQDs' PL spectra has not been understood so far. Because bottom-up routes for synthesizing CQDs have the characteristics of uncontrollability, multi-dispersion, and heterogeneity, predicting the factors affecting PL and the synergy between them is a difficult task [42]. CQDs with almost no sp2 domain are obtained depending on the symmetry of raw materials and randomness of the reaction. The variety of CQDs structures leads to internal and external factors that dominate emission, contradicting the previous reports that this mechanism was excitation-dependent [43]. The internal factors affecting PL consider the quantum confinement effect (or “size” effect or effective conjugation length, related to the sp2 domain) and the surface state [44]. For CQDs, a larger “size” reduces the bandgap with a corresponding increase in PL emission wavelength. Li et al. [45] reported that PL properties varied with particle size: small (1.2 nm) CQDs located in the UV region (~350 nm), medium-sized (1.5–3 nm) CQDs located in the visible region (400–700 nm), and large (3.8 nm) CQDs located in the near-infrared region (~800 nm). However, Miao et al. [46] synthesized CQDs under controlled thermal pyrolysis and observed that PL was particle size independent. The luminosity of CQDs can also be adjusted by controlling the surface state, which takes into account both the surface configurations (functional groups with different oxidation degrees, defects, edge states, polymers) and the doping heteroatoms (such as nitrogen, fluorine, sulfur, phosphorus, or selenium). Changes in the chemical composition of CQDs, through dehydrogenation reactions that occur during carbonization, lead to dots with either more oxygen-containing or nitrogen-containing groups [43,44]. The energy gap in the CQDs not only relies on the total level of N and O content but also on the relative amount of different types of carbon bonding with N and O [44]. New absorption bandgaps are gradually created as the O content increases in CQDs structure, enabling tunable photoluminescence. Specific functional groups in CQDs structure, such as CO, CO, COOH, and OCOH, produce many distinct energy levels. Electron-donating hydroxyl groups on CQDs promote PL, while electron-withdrawing groups, such as carboxyl groups, absorb part of the radiation signal, weakening the PL. Miao et al. [46] proposed that the increasement of surface COOH groups concentration caused red-shifted PL emissions. It has already been reported that the blue emission in the range of 430–446 nm is due to the electron-hole recombination or quantum size effect. In contrast, the green emission in the range of 510 nm is related to the presence of surface defect states [47]. As for external factors dominating emission, it can be highlighted fluorophore molecules formed from certain raw materials that remain attached to the carbon core of CQDs and environmental factors [42]. Among the environmental effects that can alter emission sites and electronic energy levels, the particle-solvent interactions in synthesis and dissolution, the temperature influence on particle aggregation, and pressure variations changing the CQDs' structure can be cited. Considering all these influences, it is concluded that the controllable preparation of CQDs remains crucial for understanding the emission behavior of these particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion of CQDs in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The solution behavior of CQDs was investigated in twenty solvents with distinct physicochemical characteristics, including protic/aprotic polar and nonpolar ones. The solvent was considered incompatible or unsuitable if sedimentation of CQDs powder was observed after ultrasonic treatment. Solvents capable of clearly dispersing CQDs after one hour of sonication were classified as compatible or suitable (Table 2 ). After ultrasonic method, stable dispersions were obtained in acetone (ACE), benzyl alcohol (BzOH), chloroform (CHCl3), dichloromethane (DCM), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), octan-1-ol, propan-2-ol, pyridine (PY), and tetrahydrofuran (THF). In these solvents, no sedimentation was observed over time, even after three months. For example, when CQDs were dispersed in acetone, the dispersion presented a zeta potential of −15.5 ± 3.7 mV. Therefore, these solvents were considered appropriate solvents for the spirulina-derived CQDs.

In contrast, most nonpolar solvents and some polar solvents like water, ethanol (EtOH), and methanol (MeOH) resulted in poor dispersion of CQDs and were classified as unsuitable for determining the HSPs. When CQDs were dispersed in water, a zeta potential of −2.2 ± 3.8 mV was obtained. An interesting feature is that for times less than one-hour post-sonication CQDs showed good solubility in all tested solvents, including good PL, as presented in Fig. 6 . Contrarily, for unsuitable solvents (Fig. 6a), it is possible to note a shift in the PL emission peak, probably due to the aggregation of nanoparticles. In Fig. 6b, this effect is not observed. Fig. 6c shows a photograph of the CQDs' solutions under 360 nm illumination. The flasks' order follows the same presented in Table 2, i.e., the first flask refers to hexadecane, and the last one refers to THF.

The results of the CQDs' dispersion tests are shown in Table 2 and were used to determine the HSP values for the nanoparticles. The HSPs of each tested solvent were obtained from the literature [30,35]. In all cases, δD, δP, δH, and R0 values are expressed in MPa1/2 units. The dispersibility of the tested solvents was arbitrarily classified as one (1) for solvents that provided suitable CQDs dispersions or zero (0) for unsuitable solvents. This experimental data was fitted further to a sphere following the procedure described in [30]. The δD, δP, δH, and R0 values for CQDs were estimated using the fminsearch solver algorithm implemented in MATLAB® [35].

The values of δD, δP, δH, and R0 obtained with fminsearch solver were 19.21, 9.76, 7.43, and 7.619, respectively. This algorithm could find the best solutions since the values of the 1–data_fit function were 0. This means that all suitable solvents are located inside the HSP sphere while the nonsuitable solvents lay outside, as shown in Fig. 7 . This is also seen in Table 2 from the relative energy difference (RED) values (RED <1 = strong interaction between the CQDs and the solvent while RED >1 = weak interaction between the two substances). The HSPs and R0 values found here were similar to solubility parameters reported for other carbon-based nanomaterials [30,48,49]. Another similarity with the literature is related to structural features of as-prepared CQDs and the small diameter (c.a. 10.6 nm), which directly influences the number of π-π interactions, and consequently, the δD term [50] and the dispersion in common solvents.

Few reports discuss the dispersibility of CQDs in different solvents, a behavior directly related to some factors, such as the presence of hydrophilic, hydrophobic, or amphiphilic regions on the particle surface, particle size, pH, and capping agents [51]. According to the solubility parameters reported in Table 2, the good solvents for the produced CQDs were those with hydrogen-bond contributions (δH) below 14 MPa1/2. As the δH is related to the number of functional groups on the particle surface that form hydrogen bonds, this dispersion behavior becomes an indication of a smaller amount of polar groups in these CQDs structure [30]. The pyrolysis causes the loss of some oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups (OH, COOH, and NH2) initially presented in the organic matter [25,27]. The FTIR results confirmed this partial loss of polar functional groups present in the biochar structure after pyrolysis. CQDs with hydrophobic characteristics were highly dispersed in acetone, chloroform, toluene, tetrahydrofuran (THF), hexane, cyclohexane, dimethylformamide (DMF) and acetonitrile [51]. The most common approach for the production of water-soluble CQDs is based on hydrothermal treatments of water-soluble precursors [52]. Processes and treatments that enrich the surfaces of CQDs with amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups favor their water solubility. The solubility of these materials in solvents and their potential applications are related to the types and extent of surface functional groups upon their structure. Unfortunately, how to precisely control the surface functionalization of CQDs during the synthetic process is also an exasperating problem [53]. Here, we studied the solubility sphere for these materials to predict future particular applications and even the possibility of tunning CQDs with a hydrophobic character into those with a hydrophilic character and vice versa. In addition, the greater the solubility of CQDs in a particular solvent, the stronger its photoluminescent emission. CQDs with a less hydrophilic behavior would be more advantageous for an agricultural application considering preparing solid-state materials (films or powders) that can be stored and transported [10]. The possibility of converting the surface properties of CQDs enables their use as delivering matrices of specific molecules (such as pesticides, nutrients, plant growth regulators, etc.) into plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs on lentil plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: Several studies have shown that CQDs derived from different precursors have a beneficial effect on the growth of plants such as mung bean sprouts, rice, peanut plants, grasses, and others, without posing any threat to food security [5–9]. Compared with the lignocellulosic biochar, the algae-derived biochar has a smaller surface area and carbon content but a greater cation exchange capacity. The higher pH values of algae biochars can adjust acidified soils, while their higher nitrogen content, inorganic components, and ashes are advantageous for soil balance in agriculture [38]. To investigate whether spirulina-derived CQDs affect the growth and development of plants, lentil seeds were chosen to be treated with these nanoparticles. The lentil seeds were cultivated with different concentrations of CQDs aqueous solution (0–0.5 mg/mL) (experiment group) and pure water (control group). Seed germination, root, and stem lengths sprout height, and fresh weight of the plants were recorded in 6 days old-lentil seedlings. These plant growth variables were chosen following the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency guideline for nanotoxicity assay of plants [54]. Fig. 8a shows the lentil seedlings' phenotypes detected at the end of the experiment (day 6), according to the CQDs treatments regarding control (deionized water). It can be seen that the presence of CQDs contributed to increasing the overall growth of lentil seedlings and that this increase was dependent on the concentration of CQDs solution. Among the CQDs-treated plants, the best results were observed for solutions with concentrations of 0.05 mg/mL and 0.1 mg/mL. CQDs solutions with concentrations higher than 0.2 mg/mL constrained the plantule's growth. As shown in Fig. 8b–e, the root, and seedling lengths and biomass of lentil sprouts exposed to the 0.1 mg/mL CQD solution increased by 48%, 105%, and 120%, respectively, when compared to the control.

The enhancements effects of CQDs on crop plants' growth are concentration-dependent, i.e., CQDs at a moderate dose contribute to crop growth, while a higher dose inhibits it [10]. At an optimal amount, CQDs can accelerate the photosynthesis and the nutrition assimilation in plants, besides enhancing the resistance of crops to abiotic/biotic stresses due to their intrinsic free radical scavenging properties. In addition, CQDs have broad-spectrum antibacterial activity against bacteria and fungi. In contrast, CQDs at high concentrations caused oxidative damage (higher H2O2 content and lipid peroxidation) when exposed to maize plants, altering their enzymes activities [55]. The exposure of maize plants to 1000 mg/L and 2000 mg/L CQDs solutions significantly reduced their roots and shoot fresh weights [56]. For the growth of rice seeds, the optimal dose of CQDs solution was 0.56 mg/mL, being this value correlated to the oxygen functional groups content in CQDs [8]. The CQDs enter into the plants and are transported from the root via the stem to the leaf, then penetrate the plant cell and plant nuclear membranes and may diffuse to any part of the plant cell due to their small size [8,24,38,57]. Nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped CQDs derived from algae biomass quickly entered the root hairs and lateral roots [58]. The oxygen content of CQDs represents the hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) on these particle's surfaces, which can easily combine with water molecules to enter the seeds and accelerate plant growth. The moisture level of the treated seeds is a critical factor for seed germination.

CQDs treatment also improves the efficiency of plant photosynthesis, probably due to their photo-induced electron-donating and accepting properties. In a simulated in vivo environment, the CQDs could be degraded into plant hormone analogs (only for graphitic-based CQDs) and CO2 by the enzyme horseradish peroxidase (HRP) at the presence of H2O2 [8]. The possible degradation mechanism of CQDs was described as follows: initially, due to the fact of oxygen-containing groups on its surface, which becomes negatively charged in aqueous suspension (negative Zeta potential value), the particles orient themselves toward a positively charged arginine residue (Arg178), near the site of HRP. Then, the CQDs' carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are more easily oxidized by the HRP. Su et al. [6] also reported the positive effect of using CQDs on peanut growth under natural soil conditions. The peanut plants irrigated with CQDs presented increased fruit productivity. Further studies are necessary to understand the effect of these nanoparticles on other physiological plant indices (nitrate content, chlorophyll content, intercellular CO2 concentration, etc.) [57].

Here, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained by pyrolysis from microalgae spirulina without any surface passivating agent. CQDs with ~10 nm average size and evidenced blue photoluminescence emission around 450 nm under 340–400 nm excitation wavelength with a 23.5% quantum yield when produced at 300 °C for 2 h. Based on thermodynamic and kinetic parameters, it was inferred that spirulina pyrolysis occurs slowly and non-spontaneously, with a mean activation energy of 192.6 kJ/mol. Besides presenting a wide range of solubility, the synthesized nanoparticles showed efficiency in lentil seeds growth, potentially applicable as biostimulants for sustainable agriculture.

Lucas Dall Agnol: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Roberta Motta Neves: Methodology, Data curation. Marcelo Maraschin: Methodology, Supervision. Sidnei Moura: Supervision. Heitor Luiz Ornaghi Jr: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Fernanda Trindade Gonzalez Dias: Writing – review & editing. Otávio Bianchi: Investigation, Supervision.
